ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for pumpkin rice; mapped to broader 'rice dish' class (FOODON_03315595). A more specific term for vegetable-mixed rice dishes does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Rice dishes) used as the closest defensible broader category. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for pumpkin rice in the current FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for pumpkin rice as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of pumpkin rice is approximately 200 g (one rice bowl), which is a standard portion size for a rice-based main dish in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Pumpkin Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pumpkin Rice (F05100110). All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are taken verbatim from the HPB record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked white rice with vegetable (closest analogue) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pumpkin rice as a composite dish. GI of 72 is estimated using white rice (jasmine/long-grain, cooked) as the dominant carbohydrate contributor, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database values for cooked white rice (GI range 64–80, representative value 72). Pumpkin has a GI of approximately 75 but contributes a small carbohydrate mass relative to rice in this dish. The composite GI is therefore anchored at 72 (white rice dominant). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 36.25 × 2.0) / 100 = 52.2 for a 200 g serving (carbohydrate per serving = 72.5 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 329 mg/100 g; a 200 g serving delivers approximately 658 mg sodium, representing 29–44% of the recommended daily sodium limit (1500–2300 mg/day).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit pumpkin rice to a half-bowl portion (100 g), request reduced soy sauce during preparation, and pair the dish with potassium-rich vegetables such as spinach or sweet potato to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) and high glycemic load per serving (~52.2) driven by white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source; dietary fibre at only 1.28 g/100 g provides insufficient buffering of postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit pumpkin rice made with white rice; substituting brown rice or a 50:50 brown-to-white rice blend and reducing portion size to 100 g paired with non-starchy vegetables and a lean protein source can substantially lower the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 329 mg/100 g elevates blood pressure risk; saturated fat is low (~0.38 g/100 g) and total fat is modest (1.75 g/100 g), which is favourable, but the refined-carbohydrate base raises triglyceride and LDL risk with regular high-portion consumption.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, pumpkin rice should be consumed in portions no larger than 150 g, prepared with low-sodium soy sauce, and accompanied by omega-3-rich foods or vegetables high in soluble fibre to mitigate the atherogenic risk from the refined-carbohydrate and sodium profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined white rice drives high glycemic load and may promote pro-inflammatory mediators; dietary fibre at 1.28 g/100 g is insufficient to support gut microbiome modulation relevant to immune regulation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute white rice with brown rice or quinoa to reduce glycemic load, increase the pumpkin proportion to benefit from beta-carotene antioxidant activity, and use low-sodium soy sauce to keep sodium within the recommended anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pumpkin rice (南瓜饭, Nasi labu) is a Singaporean rice-based dish consisting of white rice cooked together with diced or cubed pumpkin pieces, typically seasoned with soy sauce and sometimes garlic or sesame oil. The dish is prepared by steaming or boiling the rice and pumpkin together so that the pumpkin softens and partially breaks down, imparting a mildly sweet, earthy flavour and a golden-orange hue to the rice. The texture is soft and slightly moist, with the pumpkin pieces ranging from tender chunks to a near-purée depending on preparation style. Nutritionally, pumpkin rice provides 173 kcal per 100 g, with 36.25 g carbohydrate, 3.56 g protein, and 1.75 g fat. Sodium is elevated at 329 mg/100 g due to soy sauce seasoning, and dietary fibre is modest at 1.28 g/100 g. The dish has a high glycemic index (~72) driven by white rice, making it a high-glycemic-load food that requires careful portion management for individuals with type 2 diabetes or insulin resistance. Pumpkin contributes beta-carotene (a vitamin A precursor) and modest potassium, partially offsetting the sodium load. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-suitable, and is commonly consumed as a main meal component at hawker centres and home kitchens across Singapore. It is not suitable for gluten-free diets due to soy sauce containing wheat. Clinical guidance recommends substituting brown rice to improve fibre content and lower the glycemic response, and reducing soy sauce to manage sodium intake for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients.

Is pumpkin rice good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F05100110) at a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy as a close analogue (documented). GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using white rice as the dominant carbohydrate contributor, which is a defensible and documented analogous estimate.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.38 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked white rice with vegetable seasoning, as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item. This is documented in nutrient_source. 2. The following fields are set to -1 (data not available in HPB or USDA for this specific composite dish): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profiles for this specific Singaporean pumpkin rice preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for pumpkin rice as a composite dish exists in the approved GI source database. 4. All core macros (protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required. 5. Soy sauce is listed as an allergen source (contains wheat/gluten); the dish is not gluten-free.
